Regional concentration in eastus and westus2: storage and many networking/compute resources are concentrated in eastus and westus2 (storage eastus ~24%, many VMs and public IPs in westus2 ~24%), so plan the finance web app and its database in the same region (eastus or westus2) to minimize latency and inter-region egress costs.

Private networking adoption: the tenant already uses private connectivity primitives (56 Private Endpoints and 74 Private DNS zones), so design the internal finance web app and Azure SQL backend with VNet integration, Private Endpoints and private DNS zone links to keep traffic on the private network.

Key Vault protective defaults: Key Vaults overwhelmingly have soft-delete (enableSoftDelete) and long retention (90 days) with purge protection enabled, so store DB credentials and TLS certificates in Key Vault and align rotation/recovery procedures while enabling RBAC-based access (currently enablerbacauthorization is False for ~60% of vaults) for tighter access control.

App Service sizing and access patterns: App Service plans and sites commonly use Basic SKUs (B1 ~29%) and public network access is frequently Enabled while managed identities are widely used, so for an internal finance app choose an appropriately sized plan (upgrade from B1 if load requires), assign a managed identity, and restrict public access via VNet integration or a Private Endpoint.

Relational DB conventions: existing Azure SQL databases lean toward vCore/Hyperscale configurations (Hyperscale ~60% among databases) and databases use user-assigned identities, so consider Azure SQL vCore/Hyperscale with a user-assigned managed identity for scale and automation needs and place the server behind a Private Endpoint (many SQL servers today still have publicnetworkaccess enabled ~57%).

Web perimeter security is standardized: Application Gateways are consistently deployed as WAF_v2 and Front Door WAF Premium is used across the tenant, so expose the finance web app through a WAF-enabled perimeter (Application Gateway WAF_v2 or Front Door Premium) and integrate it with internal load balancing to enforce TLS and OWASP protections.

Subnet and VNet naming conventions: virtual networks frequently include subnets named 'default', 'appSubnet' and 'endpointSubnet' (top subnet names ~24%, 6.5%, 6.5%), so align the finance deployment to those subnet naming and peering conventions and apply corresponding NSG rules to match operational practices.

Managed identity usage is widespread: many platform resources already use SystemAssigned or UserAssigned identities (VMs and platform services show high identity usage), so assign a managed identity to the web app and grant least-privilege access to SQL and Key Vault to enable credentialless access and easier automation.

Mixed public network posture across services: several services have publicnetworkaccess enabled (Key Vault ~92%, Storage accounts Enabled ~54%) while a significant minority are locked down, so explicitly set publicnetworkaccess to Disabled for the finance app's storage and backups and use Private Endpoints or service endpoints to enforce internal-only access.
